findings.reachability: Requires a third-party plugin or theme to hook 'leadin_view_plugin_menu_capability' or 'leadin_connect_plugin_capability' and return a weaker capability (e.g., 'read'). If that happens, all leadin/v1 REST endpoints become accessible to any authenticated subscriber.
findings.justification: Both permission callbacks delegate the capability string to apply_filters(), returning defaults 'edit_posts' and 'manage_options' respectively; any WordPress code that hooks 'leadin_view_plugin_menu_capability' or 'leadin_connect_plugin_capability' can lower the required role to 'read', granting arbitrary authenticated users access to all plugin REST routes.

detection_pattern.section_name: Capability Downgrade via Filterable Permission Callback
detection_pattern.description: Catches REST permission_callback implementations that pass an apply_filters() return value directly into current_user_can(). If a third party hooks the filter and supplies a weaker capability string, all routes guarded by this callback become accessible to lower-privileged users.

findings.location: verify_permissions() docblock line 52-56, verify_admin_permissions() docblock line 62-66
findings.reachability: Not directly exploitable, but can mislead developers into removing or bypassing capability checks in subclasses, believing the nonce is sufficient authorization.
findings.justification: The docblock states 'User permissions are already verified by nonce wp_rest automatically,' which is factually incorrect: the wp_rest nonce authenticates the request as originating from a logged-in WordPress session but does NOT perform capability/role authorization; this false claim could lead developers to omit proper capability checks in derived controllers.

detection_pattern.description: Detects code comments claiming a nonce check substitutes for authorization. WordPress nonces authenticate session origin, not user capabilities; treating them as authorization is a known anti-pattern that leads to privilege escalation when capability checks are later removed.
